SORT: Simple Online and Realtime Tracking

Implementing pedestrian tracking with YOLO detections, Kalman filter prediction, IoU matching, and the Hungarian algorithm

by Lucky Beulla Muhoza & Theo Demetriades

This page documents our implementation of a simple multi-object tracking pipeline based on SORT (Simple Online and Realtime Tracking). Our main goal was to track pedestrians across video frames by combining object detection, motion prediction, and matching. In our project, the objects of interest were pedestrians, and we used pretrained models designed to detect them in images and video.

The overall tracking pipeline consists of four main steps:

  • Object detection using a pretrained YOLO model from OpenCV
  • Motion prediction with a Kalman filter
  • IoU calculation and generation of an IoU matrix
  • Matching detections to tracks with the Hungarian algorithm

Object Detection

The first step of the pipeline is detecting pedestrians in each frame of the video. For this part, we used a pretrained YOLO model from OpenCV.

For each frame, the detector outputs bounding boxes around pedestrians. These detections are then passed into the tracking stage.

Kalman Filter Prediction

After detection, the next step is prediction. Each active track is represented by a Kalman filter, which estimates where an object is likely to appear in the next frame based on its previous position and motion.

This helps the tracker remain stable from frame to frame, especially when detections are not perfectly smooth. The Kalman filter provides a predicted bounding box for each existing track, which can then be compared with the new detections in the current frame.

IoU Calculation and IoU Matrix

To decide which detection belongs to which predicted track, we measure the overlap between bounding boxes using Intersection over Union (IoU). IoU is the ratio of the area of overlap between two boxes to the total area covered by both boxes.

A high IoU means that a detection and a predicted track likely correspond to the same object. A low IoU means that they probably do not match.

For every frame, we compute IoU values between:

  • the predicted bounding boxes from the tracker, and
  • the new detection bounding boxes from YOLO.

These values are organized into an IoU matrix, where each row represents an existing track and each column represents a new detection. This matrix is the basis for the matching step.

Hungarian Algorithm Matching

Once the IoU matrix is built, we use the Hungarian algorithm to find the best one-to-one assignment between predicted tracks and new detections. The purpose of this step is to pair each track with the most appropriate detection while considering the overall matching quality across all objects in the frame.

In our implementation, detections with strong overlap are matched to existing tracks. If a detection does not match any current track, it may start a new track. If an existing track is not matched for too long, it may be removed.

Detections tell us where pedestrians are in the current frame, the Kalman filter predicts where tracked pedestrians should appear next, IoU measures overlap, and the Hungarian algorithm decides the best assignments.

Testing and Results

Single Object Test

We first tested the detection and tracking pipeline on a simple video containing only one person. At this stage, we had already implemented detections, the Kalman filter, and IoU calculation.

In this setting, the predicted boxes followed the object smoothly, and the IoU stayed above 0.9, which indicates strong overlap between predictions and detections. This showed that the pipeline worked well in a simple one-object scenario.

Two-Person Crossing Test

We then moved to a more difficult test: a video with two people walking past each other. This added the challenge of maintaining the correct identity for each person when they came close together.

In this setting, we observed ID switches. Two main behaviors appeared:

  1. The two tracks stayed active, but their IDs swapped when the people crossed.
  2. One ID was lost, and a new track was created for one of the pedestrians, while the other maintained its ID.

This showed that once objects become close or partially overlap, matching based only on IoU can become ambiguous.

MOT17 Benchmark Evaluation

To test the tracker in a more realistic setting, we evaluated it on a sequence from the MOT17 benchmark, which is a standardized dataset used to measure how well multi-object tracking algorithms perform on real-world video sequences.

We ran our implementation on a sequence containing multiple pedestrians. In this more complex scenario, we saw that when the tracker matched correctly, the box overlap was reasonable. However, the tracker still had clear limitations.

The main issues we observed were:

  • Missed detections: objects that were not matched to any track
  • ID switches: cases where the tracker assigned the wrong identity after occlusion or crossing

These results showed that the main challenge was not simply predicting motion, but maintaining consistent identities across time.

Key Takeaway

Our biggest takeaway from this project is that maintaining identity is harder than predicting motion. The Kalman filter can give stable and useful motion predictions, but keeping the same object identity through occlusion, interaction, and crossing is much more difficult.

This is one reason why more advanced trackers often include additional cues such as appearance features or re-identification methods, instead of relying only on motion and overlap.

Project Code

You can view the full implementation of our SORT tracker on GitHub.

View the project repository on GitHub

References

A. Bewley, Z. Ge, L. Ott, F. Ramos, and B. Upcroft, “Simple Online and Realtime Tracking,” Proc. IEEE Int. Conf. Image Process. (ICIP), 2016, pp. 3464–3468. doi: 10.1109/ICIP.2016.7533003
